site.name: Linux One Liners
site.tagline: Fix Linux problems with commands you can inspect first.
site.description: Short, copyable Linux commands with safety notes, when-not-to-use guidance, and simulated output from disposable demo environments.

build_status.now_building: fourteen issue-focused lessons with visible simulated output, owned analytics, and isolated demo vessels
next_up: publish ten Linux Survival Basics lessons to test search demand
add web hosting, DNS, SSL, and VPS troubleshooting fixes
add comments with SSO after moderation controls are ready
collect first-party page, click, copy, scroll, referrer, viewport, and timing events
expand useful pages into short videos and deeper troubleshooting paths

series.title: Linux Survival Basics
series.summary: The commands to inspect a machine before guessing.
series.audience: new Linux users, analysts, junior admins, and self-hosters

series.title: Web Server Rescue
series.summary: Small checks that separate DNS, TLS, Nginx, and app failures.
series.audience: site owners, developers, and solo operators

series.title: Dangerous Commands
series.summary: Commands worth understanding before copying from the internet.
series.audience: anyone with shell access to a system they care about

series.title: Cybersecurity Triage
series.summary: Defensive commands for checking exposure, logs, permissions, and suspicious activity.
series.audience: site owners, students, junior analysts, and defensive operators

series.title: Hosting Operations
series.summary: Web hosting, SSL, DNS, Nginx, deployment, backups, and VPS management.
series.audience: solo founders, developers, and small site operators

series.title: Apple Terminal
series.summary: macOS and Apple-adjacent terminal workflows for developers and power users.
series.audience: Mac users who need practical terminal fixes

lessons.title: Your Site Is Not Down. DNS Might Be Lying.
lessons.hook: The browser said the site was gone. The server was answering fine.
lessons.problem: A domain appears unreachable, but you need to separate a real server outage from stale or wrong DNS.
lessons.starting_state: A domain lookup may point somewhere unexpected, while the target VPS still serves the site.
lessons.what_changed: The request bypasses normal DNS for one call and tests the HTTPS server directly.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this when DNS, browser cache, router DNS, or CDN routing may be hiding the real server state.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not treat a successful forced request as proof that public DNS is correct.
lessons.undo: No system state is changed. Remove the forced IP and test normal DNS again.
lessons.expected_output: HTTP response headers or page content from the target server.

shorts.title: Your server is not down. DNS is lying.
shorts.voiceover: Before you blame Nginx, force one request straight to the VPS. If that works, the server is alive and DNS is the suspect.
shorts.caption: One command separates DNS failure from server failure.

linkedin.question: When a site disappears, do you check DNS first or the web server first?

youtube.description: A quick Linux one-liner for testing whether DNS is lying about a live web server.

experiments.variant_a: Your server is not down. DNS is lying.
experiments.variant_b: Before you blame Nginx, run this.

lessons.title: Find the Files Eating Your Disk
lessons.hook: The disk was full, but guessing at folders was the slow part.
lessons.problem: A machine is low on disk space and you need to quickly find the largest files under a path.
lessons.starting_state: Disk usage is high and broad directory totals are not specific enough.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists large files so cleanup is based on evidence.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before deleting logs, caches, backups, or uploads.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Avoid running broad searches on extremely busy systems without considering IO impact.
lessons.expected_output: A sorted list of byte sizes and file paths.

shorts.title: Find the files eating your disk.
shorts.voiceover: Do not start deleting random folders. First list the largest files, then decide what is actually safe to remove.
shorts.caption: Find large files before deleting anything.

linkedin.question: What is the first disk-space command you usually run?
linkedin.cta: Full command and safer cleanup notes

youtube.description: A quick command for finding large files before guessing at disk cleanup.

experiments.variant_a: The disk was full, but guessing at folders was the slow part.
experiments.variant_b: This is the command I run before deleting anything.

lessons.title: Run Rsync Without Deleting Your Backup
lessons.hook: One rsync flag can save you. Another can erase the wrong side.
lessons.problem: You need to preview an rsync operation before moving or deleting files.
lessons.starting_state: A source directory and backup directory may differ.
lessons.what_changed: The dry run prints intended changes without modifying files.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before any sync involving deletes, production data, or backups.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not assume dry-run output is safe if the source and destination paths are reversed.
lessons.undo: No state is changed during the dry run. If you remove `-n`, make a backup first.
lessons.expected_output: A list of files that would be copied, updated, or deleted.

shorts.title: Preview rsync before it bites.
shorts.voiceover: The `-n` flag is the pause button. It shows what rsync would do before you let it touch the files.
shorts.caption: Dry-run destructive syncs first.

linkedin.question: Have you ever seen `rsync --delete` pointed the wrong way?

youtube.description: A short demo showing why rsync dry runs matter before using --delete.

experiments.variant_a: One rsync flag can save you. Another can erase the wrong side.

lessons.title: Watch Logs Without Opening the Whole File
lessons.hook: The app was failing now. Opening a giant log file was the wrong move.
lessons.problem: You need to watch recent log lines while a service or script is actively failing.
lessons.starting_state: A log file is large or changing quickly, and you only need recent lines.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command streams the end of the file so new failures are visible immediately.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this while reproducing a web server, script, cron, or service failure.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not leave noisy log tails running forever on a shared terminal.
lessons.undo: Press Ctrl-C to stop following the file.
lessons.expected_output: Recent log lines followed by new lines as they are written.

shorts.title: Watch the failure happen live.
shorts.voiceover: Do not open the whole log file. Tail the last useful lines, reproduce the issue, and watch the error arrive.
shorts.caption: Use `tail -f` while reproducing the bug.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect logs before or after reproducing a bug?

youtube.description: A quick demo for watching live log output without opening a giant file.

lessons.title: Find Errors Before Reading Every Log Line
lessons.hook: The error was in the log. The problem was finding it without reading noise.
lessons.problem: A log file has too much output and you need a quick first pass for failure words.
lessons.starting_state: A log exists, but most lines are not relevant to the current failure.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters for common failure terms and shows the most recent matches.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this as a first pass before deeper log parsing.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not assume no matches means no failure. Logs may use different wording.
lessons.expected_output: Recent matching lines containing likely failure terms.

shorts.title: Find the failure words first.
shorts.voiceover: Before reading every line, filter for the words that usually matter: error, failed, denied, and timeout.
shorts.caption: Filter noisy logs before deep reading.

linkedin.question: What failure words do you grep for first?

youtube.description: A simple grep pattern for finding likely error lines in noisy logs.

experiments.variant_a: Find errors before reading every log line.
experiments.variant_b: This grep pattern cuts through noisy logs.

lessons.title: Check What Is Actually Listening
lessons.hook: The app was running. The port was not listening.
lessons.problem: A service appears started, but you need to confirm whether anything is listening on the expected port.
lessons.starting_state: A service status may say running, while the network port may still be closed or bound elsewhere.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists listening sockets and the owning process when available.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this when debugging web server, API, SSH, or database connectivity.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not expose closed services just because a port is not listening; check config first.
lessons.expected_output: Listening TCP or UDP sockets with local addresses, ports, and process names.

shorts.title: Running is not the same as listening.
shorts.voiceover: A service can look alive and still not answer on the port you expect. Check the socket before chasing the wrong layer.
shorts.caption: Confirm the port before blaming the app.

linkedin.question: When a service is up but unreachable, do you check the port or the logs first?

youtube.description: A quick command for checking whether a Linux service is actually listening on a port.

experiments.variant_a: The app was running. The port was not listening.
experiments.variant_b: Running is not the same as listening.

lessons.title: Inspect Permissions Before Changing Them
lessons.hook: The permission fix was easy. Knowing what not to chmod was the hard part.
lessons.problem: A file or directory has a permission issue, but recursive fixes could break more than they repair.
lessons.starting_state: A path may fail because one parent directory, owner, or mode blocks access.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command shows permissions for each path component.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before `chmod -R` or `chown -R` so you know which layer actually blocks access.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not use inspection as the final fix; it only identifies where the permission problem is.
lessons.expected_output: A component-by-component permission and owner listing for the path.

shorts.title: Inspect before you chmod.
shorts.voiceover: Recursive chmod is tempting because it is fast. Inspect the path first so you fix the layer that is actually blocking access.
shorts.caption: Find the permission problem before changing permissions.

linkedin.question: What permission command do you trust before reaching for `chmod -R`?

youtube.description: A safer first step for Linux permission debugging before recursive chmod or chown.

experiments.variant_b: This is what I run before `chmod -R`.

lessons.title: Find the Exact Log Line Before You Scroll
lessons.hook: The error was there. The useful part was knowing exactly where it was.
lessons.problem: A log file contains likely failures, but you need line numbers so you can inspect context around the match.
lessons.starting_state: A log file has multiple warnings and errors mixed with normal service output.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints matching lines with line numbers for follow-up inspection.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this when a first-pass grep finds failures and you need the surrounding context next.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not assume these four words catch every failure; applications often log custom error language.
lessons.expected_output: Matching log lines prefixed by their line numbers.

shorts.title: Find the exact log line first.
shorts.voiceover: Line numbers make log debugging faster. First find the failure, then inspect the few lines around it instead of scrolling forever.
shorts.caption: Use `grep -n` before reading the whole log.

linkedin.question: Do you jump straight into logs, or search for line numbers first?

youtube.description: A short demo for using grep line numbers before inspecting log context.

experiments.variant_a: Find the exact log line before you scroll.
experiments.variant_b: Use `grep -n` before reading the whole log.

lessons.title: Find Which Folder Is Filling the Disk
lessons.hook: The disk was full. The fastest clue was the folder, not the file.
lessons.problem: A server is low on disk and you need a quick folder-level view before drilling into files.
lessons.starting_state: Disk pressure is visible, but the largest top-level directory is unknown.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints human-readable folder sizes and sorts them.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before deeper `find` searches or cleanup decisions.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Avoid assuming the largest folder is safe to delete; size is not ownership or importance.
lessons.expected_output: A sorted list of directory sizes under `/var`.

shorts.title: Find the folder filling your disk.
shorts.voiceover: Start broad. Find the folder that is growing before you chase individual files.
shorts.caption: Folder-level disk triage first.

linkedin.question: Do you start disk cleanup with `du` or `find`?

youtube.description: A quick folder-level disk usage command for Linux cleanup triage.

experiments.variant_a: Find which folder is filling the disk.
experiments.variant_b: Start disk cleanup with this broad check.

lessons.title: Show Only Recent Errors
lessons.hook: The log had old failures too. I only cared about the newest ones.
lessons.problem: A noisy log contains old and new errors, and you need the most recent likely failures.
lessons.starting_state: The log has multiple historical messages mixed together.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters likely failures and keeps the latest matches.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this during incident triage when recent failures matter more than old history.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a complete incident report; it intentionally drops older context.

shorts.title: Show the newest errors only.
shorts.voiceover: Old log lines can waste your time. Filter the likely errors and keep the newest matches first.
shorts.caption: Recent errors beat endless scrolling.

linkedin.question: When logs are noisy, do you filter first or tail first?

youtube.description: A short Linux command for showing recent error lines in noisy logs.

experiments.variant_b: Stop reading old failures during a new incident.

lessons.title: Preview What Rsync Would Delete
lessons.hook: `rsync --delete` is useful. It is also how people erase the wrong side.
lessons.problem: You need to know which files rsync would delete before running a real sync.
lessons.starting_state: The destination contains files that may not exist in the source.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes because `-n` performs a dry run.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before allowing rsync to delete anything.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not remove `-n` until paths and direction are confirmed.
lessons.undo: No state is changed during the dry run.
lessons.expected_output: Lines showing files that would be deleted.

shorts.title: Preview rsync deletes first.
shorts.voiceover: Before you let rsync delete files, dry-run it and isolate the delete lines.
shorts.caption: Never trust `--delete` without `-n` first.

linkedin.question: Would you run `rsync --delete` without checking the delete list first?

youtube.description: A safer rsync dry-run pattern for previewing files that would be deleted.

experiments.variant_b: This is the line I check before `rsync --delete`.

lessons.title: Check Owner and Mode in One Line
lessons.hook: The file existed. The owner and mode explained why it still failed.
lessons.problem: A path exists but access fails, and you need owner, group, and mode quickly.
lessons.starting_state: A file exists under a web path, but access may be blocked by ownership or mode.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints permissions, owner, group, and filename.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before changing file permissions or ownership.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not stop at the file if parent directories may block access.
lessons.expected_output: A compact permission and ownership line.

shorts.title: Check owner and mode first.
shorts.voiceover: Before changing permissions, print the owner, group, and mode in one compact line.
shorts.caption: Inspect permissions before editing them.

linkedin.question: Do you use `stat` or `ls -l` first for permission issues?

youtube.description: A compact stat command for checking file permissions and ownership.

experiments.variant_a: Check owner and mode in one line.
experiments.variant_b: The file existed, but the owner told the story.

lessons.title: Find the Processes Using Memory
lessons.hook: The server felt slow. Memory pressure was the first thing to rule out.
lessons.problem: You need a quick process-level view of memory usage.
lessons.starting_state: A system may be slow or under pressure, but the highest memory consumers are unknown.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists processes sorted by memory percentage.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this before killing processes or resizing a VPS.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not kill a process just because it uses memory; understand its role first.
lessons.expected_output: A process table sorted by memory usage.

shorts.title: Find memory-heavy processes.
shorts.voiceover: Before resizing a server or killing random processes, sort the process list by memory.
shorts.caption: Check memory pressure before guessing.

linkedin.question: What do you check first when a VPS feels slow?

youtube.description: A quick command for listing Linux processes by memory usage.

experiments.variant_b: Before resizing the VPS, run this.

lessons.title: Show Big Files in Human Units
lessons.hook: Byte counts are precise. Human units are faster under pressure.
lessons.problem: You need to find large files and read their sizes without mentally converting bytes.
lessons.command: find /var -type f -printf '%s %p\n' | sort -nr | head -10 | awk '{printf "%.1f MB %s\n", $1/1024/1024, $2}'
lessons.starting_state: Large file output is available in bytes, but hard to scan quickly.
lessons.what_changed: Nothing changes. The command converts byte counts into MB for quick triage.
lessons.when_to_use: Use this when disk cleanup needs fast human-readable ranking.
lessons.when_not_to_use: Do not delete files from this list without checking ownership and purpose.
lessons.expected_output: Large files listed with approximate MB sizes.

commands: find /var -type f -printf '%s %p\n' | sort -nr | head -10 | awk '{printf "%.1f MB %s\n", $1/1024/1024, $2}'

shorts.title: Show big files in MB.
shorts.voiceover: Finding large files is useful. Making the sizes readable makes the next decision faster.
shorts.caption: Human-readable file triage.

linkedin.question: Do you prefer raw bytes or human units during disk cleanup?

youtube.description: A one-liner for listing large files with human-readable MB sizes.

experiments.variant_a: Show big files in human units.
experiments.variant_b: Byte counts are precise, but MB is faster.
